ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bánh hỏi or Vietnamese thin rice vermicelli with grilled beef in betel leaf. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Rice noodles) is the closest parent. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for bánh hỏi with grilled beef in betel leaf.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Vietnamese noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of bánh hỏi lá lốt is approximately 350 g, comprising a bundle of thin rice vermicelli topped with scallion oil and crushed dried shrimp, accompanied by 3–4 grilled beef-in-betel-leaf rolls.

nutrient_source.title: Bún bò (Vietnamese beef noodle dish) and component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central: Beef, ground, 80% lean / 20% fat, cooked; Rice noodles, cooked; Scallion oil; Betel leaf
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for bánh hỏi lá lốt exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 55% cooked rice vermicelli (USDA FDC #168870), 30% grilled seasoned beef (USDA FDC #174036 ground beef 80/20 cooked, adjusted for betel leaf wrapping and grilling), 10% scallion oil, and 5% dried shrimp and condiments. Values cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese dishes. All values are per 100 g of the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to bánh hỏi (thin rice vermicelli)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bánh hỏi lá lốt as a composite dish. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles (GI ~53, low category), which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle in this dish. The protein and fat content from the beef and scallion oil components are expected to moderately attenuate the overall glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 34.2, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 18.5 × 3.5 = 64.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 53 (low) but a glycemic load of ~34 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a half-portion (approximately 175 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from grilled beef and sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides approximately 7.4 g saturated fat and 1085 mg sodium per serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose leaner beef cuts when possible, and balance the meal with fibre-rich vegetables to support lipid management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Hỏi Lá Lốt is a traditional Vietnamese dish consisting of super-fine steamed rice vermicelli bundles (bánh hỏi) served with grilled beef wrapped in betel leaves (bò lá lốt). The dish originates from southern Vietnam and is popular as a street food and restaurant specialty. The bánh hỏi noodles are distinguished by their extremely thin, tightly woven texture and delicate, slightly chewy mouthfeel, quite different from standard rice vermicelli. They are typically topped with fragrant scallion oil and crushed dried shrimp (tôm khô), which add umami depth and aromatic richness. The beef filling is seasoned with lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, sugar, and black pepper before being wrapped tightly in fresh betel leaves and grilled over charcoal or a gas flame, imparting a distinctive smoky, slightly peppery, and herbal flavour from the betel leaf. The dish is commonly served with a dipping sauce of fermented shrimp paste (mắm nêm) or a sweet-sour fish sauce (nước chấm). Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density of approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with a balanced macronutrient profile of roughly 9.8 g protein, 6.2 g fat, and 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The rice vermicelli base contributes a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~53), while the beef component provides iron, zinc, and vitamin B12. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce and dried shrimp, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish is served at Vietnamese restaurants such as Co Chung Restaurant and represents an authentic southern Vietnamese culinary tradition adapted for the Singaporean dining market.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for bánh hỏi lá lốt exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for cooked rice noodles and cooked ground beef, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles as the closest analogous carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Nutrient values are component-based estimates; no single approved-source entry exists for this composite Vietnamese dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used (USDA FoodData Central does not report meaningful vitamin D for rice noodles or typical beef preparations in this context). Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources for this dish. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported consistently across the component sources used. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; the actual GI of the composite dish may be lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion. Glycemic load per serving (350 g) = (53 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 34.2. The dish is classified as cuisine type Vietnamese despite being served in Singapore at a Vietnamese restaurant. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant's halal certification status is not verified. The food_group has been retained as 'Meat and alternatives' per the input record, which is defensible given the beef component is the primary protein source.

Verified all -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Verified ontology match_type values use only allowed strings. Confirmed multilingual coverage for Singaporean-cuisine context (English, Chinese simplified+traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese as dish origin language.
